name: Sonar
description: A mobile ambient social networking app that helped users discover friends and relevant people nearby.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Brett Martin wrote that Sonar failed after struggling to find product-market fit, mistaking feature requests for real signal, and failing to grow enough as a social network.
symptoms: false positive user feedback; engagement-growth tradeoff; competitor distraction; insufficient social graph growth
evidence_summary: The founder postmortem says Sonar had millions of downloads and strong press but failed to turn engagement and product iterations into the growth required for a durable social network.
notes: Strong primary source. Good first case for consumer social and false-signal analysis.

name: Binpress
description: A marketplace for developers to sell code libraries, SDKs, and other digital software products.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Eran Galperin wrote that the marketplace format was not a good fit for the products, developers were hard to sell to, and the concept did not scale as expected.
symptoms: chicken-and-egg marketplace problem; one-and-done buyers; hard developer monetization; limited scale
evidence_summary: The postmortem explains that Binpress reached meaningful revenue but developer products did not behave like a high-volume marketplace and the business hit a scale ceiling.
notes: High-quality founder postmortem with detailed marketplace mechanics.

name: onetool
description: A YC-backed SaaS management startup that tried to bundle and manage business software subscriptions.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Gordian Braun wrote that the original SaaS bundle model broke because vendor pricing levels varied too much and customer needs shifted toward managing existing tools.
symptoms: pricing spread across SaaS vendors; model pivot; integrations required; existing-customer stack complexity
evidence_summary: The founder postmortem says onetool's initial Setapp-for-SaaS model did not work because SaaS vendors had incompatible pricing levels and customers needed a different product than originally planned.
notes: Strong case for business model mismatch in B2B SaaS.

name: PageRank Panda
description: An SEO-focused product that helped users evaluate page rank and related website metrics.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Ryan Anderson said the project was too time-consuming, not gaining much traction, highly competitive, and advertising expenses were not driving expected growth.
symptoms: low traction; high competition; paid ads not working; time burden
evidence_summary: The Indie Hackers shutdown post directly states that the product lacked traction and that advertising expenses did not produce the expected growth in a competitive space.
notes: Small indie case; useful because it represents the kind of micro-failure famous databases usually miss.

name: SimpleDirect
description: A financing product for contractors built by a small founder-led company.
stated_failure_cause: Founder George Pu wrote that SimpleDirect conflicted with his principles, became too complex, had thin margins, depended on external APIs, and was underpriced for the level of service required.
symptoms: thin margins; external API dependency; increasing complexity; underpricing; high-touch support
evidence_summary: The founder postmortem frames the shutdown as a strategic sunset after recognizing that the product's complexity, margins, pricing, and operating model did not fit the founder's desired business principles.
notes: Recent case; keep but verify with original product shutdown post later.

name: Fundid
description: A fintech startup helping small businesses access financing and business capital products.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Stefanie Sample said rising interest rates, fintech market shifts, cap table pressure, and venture-path issues contributed to the shutdown.
symptoms: rising rates; strained cap table; fintech investor pullback; product direction pressure
evidence_summary: TechCrunch reports that Fundid closed on April 1, 2024 after market conditions changed and the founder lost faith in the venture path around lending and card products.
notes: Good fintech seed case; primary postmortem exists but should be archived or directly captured later.

name: Kite
description: An AI-powered coding assistant and autocomplete tool for software developers.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Adam Smith said Kite was too early for AI-assisted programming, the technology was not ready, the product did not monetize, and the team took too long to realize it.
symptoms: technology not ready; failure to monetize; expensive development; weak paid conversion
evidence_summary: TechCrunch quotes Kite's founder saying the company was more than 10 years too early to market and failed to monetize its AI coding assistant despite developer adoption.
notes: Strong devtool case. Funding may be above strict seed but still useful as core early-stage/venture-backed devtool failure.

name: Flowtab
description: A mobile ordering and payments app for bars that let customers order drinks from their phones.
stated_failure_cause: The company published a detailed death chronicle showing failed pivots, difficult bar acquisition, undercapitalization, and weak geographic density.
symptoms: hard venue acquisition; geographic density problem; premature app launch; undercapitalization
evidence_summary: TechCrunch's detailed account describes Flowtab's struggle to get enough bars and users in the same locations, plus the operational burden of selling into hospitality venues.
notes: Not a founder postmortem but based on founder materials and interviews; good local marketplace/payments case.

name: RewardMe
description: A digital loyalty and gamification platform for offline retail and restaurant businesses.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Yu-kai Chou wrote that RewardMe had customer interest and a large chain deal but lacked runway, could not raise fast enough, and key team members burned out before deployment and revenue could arrive.
symptoms: long enterprise sales cycle; delayed revenue collection; team burnout; insufficient runway
evidence_summary: The founder postmortem explains that RewardMe closed a large chain deal too late relative to runway, failed to secure the next round quickly, and lost execution capacity after team burnout.
notes: Schema gap: taxonomy has no sales_cycle_too_long enum yet, but this cause is important. Needs taxonomy v1.2 update.

name: Hakeema
description: A bootstrapped SaaS platform for social and public sector organizations.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Kyle Cureau said nonprofit customers needed custom platforms, turning the company into a profitable software agency rather than a scalable SaaS company.
symptoms: custom implementations; services-like work; profitable but not scalable; nonprofit customer requirements
evidence_summary: The founder post says Hakeema had revenue and retention, but customer needs pushed the team toward custom agency work instead of a scalable software company.
notes: Moved from test cases into core MVP because evidence quality and early-stage fit are strong.

name: Tutorspree
description: An online marketplace for matching students and parents with local tutors.
stated_failure_cause: Available analyses based on founder commentary identify heavy SEO dependence, Google's Panda update, trust issues, and marketplace disintermediation as major causes.
symptoms: Google algorithm exposure; one-channel acquisition; trust gap; off-platform leakage; funding pressure
evidence_summary: Tutorspree's acquisition engine depended heavily on SEO, and a Google algorithm change reportedly cut traffic sharply while the tutoring marketplace also struggled with trust and repeat transaction leakage.
notes: Good marketplace case, but use medium confidence because the founder postmortem URL is not the main stored source yet.

name: NewsTilt
description: A YC-backed platform intended to help journalists build direct online businesses around their work.
stated_failure_cause: Co-founder Paul Biggar wrote that NewsTilt failed because of internal company problems and product problems, including overpromising, underdelivering, and launching without core value proposition pieces.
symptoms: founder communication breakdown; incomplete MVP; overpromised features; weak user value; short runway
evidence_summary: The founder postmortem describes a product launched without key journalist-branding features and a company unable to recover because of internal communication and execution problems.
notes: Strong primary postmortem; useful for founder/team failure category.

name: Zen99
description: A YC-backed tax and insurance tool suite for independent contractors and gig workers.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Tristan Zier explained that contractors were not a homogeneous market, many did not self-identify as contractors, and needs differed sharply across segments.
symptoms: fragmented contractor personas; hard targeting; weak universal use case; controlled shutdown
evidence_summary: The founder postmortem frames Zen99's failure around the mistaken assumption that independent contractors formed one coherent customer segment with similar needs and reachable channels.
notes: Strong primary source; good ICP segmentation failure case.

name: Plancast
description: A social event-sharing service for broadcasting and discovering personal plans.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Mark Hendrickson wrote that Plancast stalled after early traction because events were a difficult content type, mainstream users did not adopt the behavior, and engagement did not scale.
symptoms: early adopter ceiling; low mainstream behavior change; weak engagement; unclear monetization
evidence_summary: The founder postmortem says Plancast could not grow beyond an early adopter community and could not make social event sharing compelling enough for broad repeated usage.
notes: Excellent behavior-change consumer social case.

name: Flud
description: A social newsreader app for iOS, Android, and Windows Phone.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Bobby Ghoshal told TechCrunch that Flud never found durable product-market fit or monetization, pivoted toward enterprise too late, and ran out of time before investors saw enough validation.
symptoms: consumer newsreader saturation; enterprise pivot too late; insufficient validation; ran out of runway
evidence_summary: The TechCrunch postmortem reports that Flud had press and downloads but failed to turn consumer interest or an enterprise pivot into a validated business before capital ran out.
notes: Good founder-interview case; secondary because not written directly by founder.

name: DrawQuest
description: A mobile drawing game and creative community from Canvas Networks.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Chris Poole said DrawQuest had traction but did not become a venture-backed opportunity because the team could not make the mobile monetization model work.
symptoms: downloads without enough revenue; weak in-app purchase monetization; runway pressure; pivot after Canvas
evidence_summary: TechCrunch reports that DrawQuest had users and engagement but could not monetize well enough through in-app purchases to justify more venture funding.
notes: Useful monetization case; not pure shutdown postmortem but based on founder interview and official closure context.

name: Newsberry
description: An email marketing product built by Wildbit before Postmark and Beanstalk became the team's focus.
stated_failure_cause: Wildbit wrote that Newsberry was profitable but stagnant, under-loved by the team, weakly marketed, and unable to justify continued full-time investment against better products.
symptoms: stagnant growth; weak marketing; stronger competing products; internal focus shifted; profitable but low ceiling
evidence_summary: The company postmortem says Newsberry made money but did not grow enough or fit the team's strengths and priorities, especially as Beanstalk and Postmark showed stronger momentum.
notes: Strong primary source; include as profitable-but-not-scalable failure.

name: Spreadable
description: A referral and word-of-mouth marketing product built by Grasshopper Group.
stated_failure_cause: Grasshopper said Spreadable was making money and acquiring customers but required a sales/support motion that did not align with the company's scalable product strengths.
symptoms: sales motion misfit; support burden; misaligned with core strengths; profitable but discontinued
evidence_summary: The case study says Spreadable was shut down despite revenue because its effective sales model and operating requirements did not fit Grasshopper's company model or strategic focus.
notes: Strong primary case for business-model/team-strength mismatch.

name: DataDistillr
description: An enterprise data analytics platform for querying and exploring data across multiple sources without ETL.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Charles Givre wrote that DataDistillr failed to reach product-market fit because of market changes, unclear monetization, building the wrong product, too many features, and weak selling.
symptoms: unclear monetization; too many features; weak sales; insufficient customer learning; market change
evidence_summary: The founder postmortem directly states the company failed to achieve product-market fit and identifies monetization, focus, sales, and build-measure-learn failures as contributors.
notes: Strong modern devtool/data case.

name: Tess.Design
description: An AI image marketplace that paid artists royalties for image generation in their style.
stated_failure_cause: Kapwing CEO Julia Enthoven wrote that Tess generated little revenue, could not overcome unresolved AI copyright risk, lacked artist-driven promotion, and could not justify continued focus.
symptoms: legal uncertainty; weak artist supply response; low revenue; enterprise buyer blocked by legal team; split focus
evidence_summary: The official retrospective says Tess shut down after legal ambiguity around AI copyright blocked enterprise interest and the marketplace failed to generate enough demand or artist-led distribution.
notes: Excellent current AI/legal marketplace case.

name: Everpix
description: A cloud photo storage and discovery startup that automatically organized users' photos.
stated_failure_cause: Everpix shut down after failing to secure funding or acquisition while user and subscriber growth could not cover infrastructure and payroll costs.
symptoms: loved product but slow growth; insufficient paid subscribers; high storage costs; failed acquisition talks
evidence_summary: Reporting on the shutdown says Everpix had strong product reviews and retention but not enough paying subscribers or investment to cover bills and scale the business.
notes: High-signal product-loved-business-failed case; secondary source but very detailed.

name: Wesabe
description: A personal finance management website that helped users track spending and get money advice.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Marc Hedlund wrote that Wesabe lost to Mint because Mint gave users a much easier and faster experience, while Wesabe required too much manual work and had worse aggregation.
symptoms: inferior user experience; manual user effort; better-funded competitor; slower time to value
evidence_summary: Marc Hedlund's postmortem, summarized by TechCrunch and AVC, says Wesabe was beaten by Mint because Mint solved the same job with less user effort and faster perceived value.
notes: Primary source is founder postmortem but current stored main URL is TechCrunch summary; source should be upgraded if original archive is located.

name: Patient Communicator
description: A digital health patient engagement and CRM product for medical practices.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Jeff Novich wrote that Patient Communicator relied on doctors seeing financial value in patient engagement, but the market did not adopt quickly enough and fundraising was difficult.
symptoms: doctor adoption friction; weak perceived urgency; healthcare sales difficulty; failed fundraising
evidence_summary: The founder postmortem explains that the product depended on physicians valuing patient engagement as a revenue and outcome lever, but buyer behavior and funding did not support scaling.
notes: Strong digital health case with primary source.

name: Standout Jobs
description: A recruitment and employer branding SaaS product for company career sites.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Ben Yoskovitz wrote that Standout Jobs raised too much too early, launched into a collapsing hiring market, asked for too much behavior change, and did not react fast enough.
symptoms: recession timing; hiring freeze; behavior change; premature funding; slow strategic reaction
evidence_summary: The postmortem says the paid product launched just before the 2008 hiring collapse, while the team lacked enough validation and asked customers to change recruiting behavior without enough urgency.
notes: Strong primary founder postmortem; useful timing plus premature funding case.

name: Monitor110
description: A Wall Street internet monitoring and alternative data product for financial professionals.
stated_failure_cause: Founder/investor postmortems cite unclear leadership, building too long before release, technical noise in the product, and delayed pivoting after too much capital and publicity.
symptoms: delayed launch; data quality issues; unclear leadership; too much funding before validation; late pivot
evidence_summary: The postmortem pattern says Monitor110 overbuilt before customer feedback, struggled with noisy data and leadership alignment, and ran out of time after delaying market validation.
notes: Useful but slightly larger funding than ideal; keep as core because it is pre-Series B style and evidence is strong.

name: MyFavorites
description: A consumer app for saving and sharing favorite things.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Steve Poland wrote that MyFavorites tried to solve a problem people did not strongly feel they had, and the team lost conviction after launch and feedback cycles.
symptoms: weak problem intensity; unclear user behavior; loss of team interest; consumer app ambiguity
evidence_summary: The founder postmortem says the problem was not one users strongly recognized, making the product difficult to position, motivate, and sustain.
notes: Good small consumer case for weak pain.

name: Workapp
description: A small platform product that drifted toward consultancy-style work.
stated_failure_cause: The founder wrote that the team realized they did not want to build a consultancy-type business and that the original platform product was undifferentiated.
symptoms: consultancy drift; undifferentiated platform; crowded internet market; founder loss of conviction
evidence_summary: The Indie Hackers shutdown post directly says the product was becoming consultancy-like while the platform itself was not sufficiently differentiated.
notes: Small but valuable bootstrapped case; founder source.

name: Video Scheduler
description: A small scheduling product for video workflows.
stated_failure_cause: The founder shut down Video Scheduler because it did not appear to be on a path to becoming a viable product.
symptoms: no clear viability path; small product; uncertain demand
evidence_summary: The founder's shutdown note states the product did not seem to be on a path toward viability, which supports a conservative product-market fit classification.
notes: Direct source but short evidence; keep medium confidence.

name: PageLines Platform 5
description: A WordPress website-building product that struggled after market and ecosystem shifts.
stated_failure_cause: The company announced Platform 5 was dead after changing WordPress market dynamics and the product's own direction made continued development unsustainable.
symptoms: WordPress ecosystem change; product direction issues; market shift; discontinued development
evidence_summary: The official shutdown announcement frames the product's death around ecosystem and market shifts in WordPress plus an inability to keep the product direction viable.
notes: Good platform/ecosystem case; verify exact dates later.

name: Zeda.io
description: A product management platform for feedback, roadmaps, and AI-assisted product workflows.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Prashant Mahajan discussed shutting down Zeda after struggling with product-management-tool adoption, enterprise SaaS sales, fundraising, and finding durable product-market fit.
symptoms: hard product management adoption; enterprise sales friction; insufficient PMF; cautious shutdown
evidence_summary: The founder interview describes why money did not solve adoption and PMF issues in a crowded product-management software category, leading to shutdown despite remaining capital discipline.
notes: Good recent founder-interview case, but podcast source should be supplemented with transcript or written source later.

name: RethinkDB
description: An open-source distributed document database company built around real-time application development.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Slava Akhmechet wrote that RethinkDB failed to build a sustainable business because it optimized for qualities developers admired but not the market priorities that drove adoption and monetization.
symptoms: developer love without sustainable monetization; open-source commercialization difficulty; wrong product priorities; strong competitor category dynamics
evidence_summary: The shutdown announcement and later founder postmortem show a technically respected product that could not turn open-source developer adoption into a durable commercial company.
notes: Strong devtool/open-source commercialization case.

name: Homejoy
description: An on-demand home cleaning marketplace connecting customers with cleaners.
stated_failure_cause: Reporting on Homejoy emphasizes weak retention, high customer acquisition costs, discount-driven growth, worker classification lawsuits, and the difficulty of making high-touch home services work as an on-demand marketplace.
symptoms: discount-led acquisition; poor repeat usage; contractor classification lawsuits; high-touch service quality problem
evidence_summary: Homejoy shut down after failing to raise more capital, with later analysis pointing to expensive acquisition, weak retention, and legal risk around contractor labor as core business problems.
notes: Useful on-demand marketplace case, but funding is higher than ideal; keep because failure mechanics are high-value.

name: Shyp
description: An on-demand shipping service that picked up, packed, and shipped items for consumers and small businesses.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Kevin Gibbon later acknowledged that Shyp pursued growth at all costs, targeted low-frequency consumers, and built an expensive operations-heavy model before finding a sustainable business.
symptoms: low-frequency consumer usage; warehouses and couriers; flat-fee pricing pressure; growth-before-sustainability; late B2B pivot
evidence_summary: Secondary reporting based on Gibbon's postmortem describes an on-demand service whose delightful user experience masked expensive logistics and poor repeat economics.
notes: Funding is high, but the founder-postmortem signal is strong for on-demand operations failure.

name: Sprig
description: An on-demand food startup that cooked and delivered its own prepared meals.
stated_failure_cause: Sprig's shutdown message said demand was high, but owning meal production through delivery at scale created operational complexity that the company could not overcome.
symptoms: thin margins; food production plus delivery; city operations; labor intensity; on-demand market pressure
evidence_summary: Coverage of Sprig's closure cites the CEO's message that demand existed but the complexity of owning the full meal-production and delivery stack was too hard to scale profitably.
notes: Good operational-complexity case; higher funding than ideal but strong source and category fit.

name: SpoonRocket
description: An on-demand prepared-meal delivery service that promised fast delivery of low-cost meals.
stated_failure_cause: SpoonRocket shut down after failing to raise necessary capital in a category where fast prepared-meal delivery required difficult operational economics.
symptoms: prepared food inventory; fast delivery promise; funding dependency; tough on-demand economics
evidence_summary: TechCrunch reported that SpoonRocket closed after it could not raise more capital, using the case as an example of the difficult economics behind app-based convenience services.
notes: Useful on-demand food case; evidence is secondary but clear.

name: Take Eat Easy
description: A European restaurant delivery marketplace operating bike-courier food delivery across multiple cities.
stated_failure_cause: Take Eat Easy ceased trading after failing to raise a Series C despite order growth, while competing against much better-funded delivery rivals in a capital-intensive category.
symptoms: failed Series C; 114 investor rejections; better-funded rivals; courier operations; delivery market consolidation
evidence_summary: Founder comments reported by TechCrunch and later European startup retrospectives show a business with traction but insufficient capital to survive a subsidy-heavy food delivery war.
notes: Good non-US marketplace case; classified fundraising primary because the source emphasizes capital cutoff.

name: Washio
description: An on-demand laundry and dry-cleaning pickup and delivery service.
stated_failure_cause: Washio shut down after generating revenue and orders, but the laundry pickup and delivery model carried low margins, operational scheduling complexity, and acquisition challenges.
symptoms: 24-hour turnaround burden; route logistics; low margins; labor-heavy service; failed acquisition talks
evidence_summary: TechCrunch reproduced the founders' shutdown letter and reported that Washio had millions in revenue and many orders, but the on-demand laundry model did not become a durable standalone business.
notes: Good on-demand services case; direct letter exists inside secondary article.

name: Move Loot
description: A full-service marketplace for buying and selling secondhand furniture, including pickup, storage, listing, and delivery.
stated_failure_cause: Move Loot shut down after trying to operate a high-touch furniture resale marketplace with difficult logistics, storage, delivery, and market-by-market density requirements.
symptoms: furniture pickup and delivery; inventory/storage burden; local marketplace density; full-service operational load
evidence_summary: TechCrunch reported that Move Loot closed and sold its customer list after prior issues, highlighting the burden of its full-service model for secondhand furniture resale.
notes: Useful marketplace/ops case; funding slightly high but category mechanics are strong.

name: Prim
description: A YC-backed door-to-door laundry service charging a flat rate for pickup, washing, folding, and delivery.
stated_failure_cause: Prim shut down after concluding that a same-day laundry pickup and delivery service at its price point was not financially viable.
symptoms: flat-rate laundry pricing; same-day operations; low-margin convenience service; small team course change
evidence_summary: TechCrunch published the customer shutdown email and noted that making money on pickup, wash, fold, and delivery at the advertised price was likely the central challenge.
notes: Good small YC on-demand case; short evidence but directly tied to unit economics.

name: Cherry
description: An on-demand car wash service that sent washers to clean customers' cars where they were parked.
stated_failure_cause: Cherry shut down its on-demand car wash service after struggling to make the mobile car-wash model work across operations, pricing, and demand.
symptoms: mobile labor dispatch; low-frequency service; local operations; convenience pricing pressure
evidence_summary: The company shutdown statement reported by TechCrunch shows an early on-demand local-services startup abandoning its core service after failing to make the model work.
notes: Good early on-demand case; evidence is concise, so confidence remains medium.

name: Secret
description: An anonymous social app that let users share posts with friends and nearby users without attaching their identity.
stated_failure_cause: Founder David Byttow shut Secret down because the app no longer represented his original vision after anonymous usage patterns produced harmful behavior and reputation problems.
symptoms: anonymous abuse; malicious rumors; reputational damage; rapid hype decay; founder vision mismatch
evidence_summary: Forbes and other coverage of Byttow's shutdown post report that Secret closed because actual user behavior diverged from the intended product vision, a classic anonymous-social trust failure.
notes: Was in test set; now included because Batch 003 explicitly targets trust and safety.

name: Yik Yak
description: An anonymous location-based social network popular on college campuses.
stated_failure_cause: Yik Yak's original app shut down after anonymous local posting created moderation, cyberbullying, and campus-safety problems while the product lost momentum.
symptoms: cyberbullying; harassment complaints; campus bans; moderation challenge; declining momentum
evidence_summary: TechCrunch reported the shutdown and later coverage of the relaunch explicitly connects the original failure to cyberbullying, harassment, and moderation problems in anonymous local communities.
notes: Funding is high and brand later relaunched, but original 2017 shutdown is analytically valuable for trust/safety.

name: Formspring
description: An anonymous question-and-answer social platform that helped popularize ask-me-anything style profiles.
stated_failure_cause: Formspring shut down after anonymous Q&A was copied, repositioning efforts failed, and the product faced persistent cyberbullying and moderation concerns.
symptoms: anonymous Q&A copied by competitors; bullying concerns; failed repositioning; declining independent edge
evidence_summary: TechCrunch reported that Formspring was shutting down after trying to move away from anonymous Q&A into an interest-based social network while competitors copied the format.
notes: Trust/safety is secondary rather than primary here because competitive copying and repositioning are clearer in the main source.

name: App.net
description: A paid social platform and developer API ecosystem intended as an alternative to ad-supported Twitter-like networks.
stated_failure_cause: App.net went into maintenance mode and later shut down because paid subscriptions and developer ecosystem revenue were not enough to support a full-time team.
symptoms: paid social network friction; insufficient revenue; niche developer community; maintenance mode
evidence_summary: TechCrunch reported that App.net shut down after failing to generate enough revenue to sustain staff, despite an ambitious paid alternative-social-network vision.
notes: Good pricing/willingness-to-pay case.

name: Blab
description: A live group video chat and broadcasting app that grew quickly around public conversations.
stated_failure_cause: CEO Shaan Puri said Blab had millions of users but most were not returning regularly, and the product failed to generate strong enough commercial traction.
symptoms: large signups; poor repeat usage; weak commercial use case; live video novelty decay
evidence_summary: Coverage of Puri's shutdown post says Blab grew fast but failed because users did not come back often enough and the product lacked commercial traction.
notes: Good consumer retention case with founder explanation via secondary coverage.

name: Meerkat
description: A mobile live-streaming app that let users broadcast video through Twitter distribution.
stated_failure_cause: Meerkat lost momentum after Twitter restricted its social graph access and acquired Periscope, while Facebook also entered live streaming.
symptoms: Twitter graph dependence; platform restriction; Periscope acquisition; Facebook Live competition; hype decay
evidence_summary: Coverage of Meerkat's death and founder comments show a consumer app whose distribution depended on Twitter and was rapidly undermined by platform owner competition.
notes: Good platform-risk case; source is secondary but strong.

name: Grooveshark
description: A music streaming service that let users upload and stream music online.
stated_failure_cause: Grooveshark shut down as part of a legal settlement after years of copyright litigation and acknowledged it failed to secure licenses from music rights holders.
symptoms: copyright lawsuits; missing music licenses; settlement shutdown; app and IP surrender
evidence_summary: TechCrunch reproduced Grooveshark's shutdown statement, which admitted the company failed to secure music licenses and agreed to cease operations as part of a settlement.
notes: Strong legal/regulatory pressure case, though older and somewhat later-stage.

name: 99dresses
description: A fashion marketplace where women could trade clothing items in a virtual closet model.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Nikki Durkin's postmortem describes a marketplace that faced cofounder, visa, funding, and execution problems while trying to build liquidity across geographies.
symptoms: two-sided marketplace cold start; geographic mismatch; cofounder problems; funding and visa stress
evidence_summary: Secondary summaries of Durkin's founder postmortem show a small marketplace struggling to create enough supply-demand liquidity while also facing severe team and funding constraints.
notes: Need original Medium/source archive later; useful because marketplace liquidity is underrepresented.

name: Dinnr
description: A meal-kit style service that delivered ingredients and recipes for home cooking.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Michal Bohanes wrote that Dinnr was a solution looking for a problem, with insufficient real customer need despite iteration and marketing attempts.
symptoms: weak pain; solution-first product; marketing could not fix demand; meal planning behavior friction
evidence_summary: The founder postmortem says the company lacked a real market need from the beginning, making later iteration and marketing ineffective.
notes: Strong primary founder lesson; good non-US weak-pain case.

name: Outbox
description: A mail digitization service that picked up users' physical mail, scanned it, and delivered it digitally.
stated_failure_cause: Outbox shut down its current service after low waitlist conversion, difficulty finding scalable acquisition channels, and pressure from the postal ecosystem around a behavior-changing mail product.
symptoms: under 10 percent waitlist conversion; hard scalable acquisition; postal ecosystem resistance; behavior-change friction
evidence_summary: TechCrunch reported Outbox's shutdown note saying waitlist yield was under 10 percent and outside-network marketing could not find a repeatable scalable acquisition channel.
notes: Good distribution plus institutional friction case; classify as distribution primary because source gives clear acquisition data.

name: ABBY
description: A documentation service for A/B tests and experimentation knowledge.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Andy Goldschmidt explained that ABBY would have required educating users into a workflow they did not yet urgently need.
symptoms: A/B test documentation was not urgent; user education burden; unclear willingness to pay
evidence_summary: The founder interview frames the failure around a product that could be useful in theory but lacked urgent enough demand to justify adoption and education.
notes: Good small European SaaS weak-pain case.

name: Adproval
description: A marketplace connecting bloggers and social media influencers with brands for sponsored content.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Matthew Anderson described raising about $300k and generating only modest software revenue while consulting work dominated the business.
symptoms: self-serve marketplace did not monetize; consulting revenue masked weak product business; unclear focus on revenue
evidence_summary: The founder interview shows a marketplace whose self-service software did not become a strong revenue engine, pushing the company toward consulting rather than scalable marketplace economics.
notes: Useful small marketplace business-model case.

name: Ansaro
description: A recruiting SaaS using data science and AI to improve interviewing and hiring decisions.
stated_failure_cause: Co-founder Sam Stone said Ansaro failed to achieve product-market fit after two pivots and never moved beyond services-heavy pilots.
symptoms: enterprise pilots without repeatable SaaS adoption; services revenue; high burn; two pivots
evidence_summary: The founder interview says Ansaro raised $3M, had enterprise customers, but failed to get past pilot contracts and returned remaining capital to investors.
notes: Strong B2B SaaS enterprise-pilot failure case.

name: Delite
description: A SaaS platform for B2B wholesale order forms and payments.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Pat Walls said Delite was a nice-to-have for wholesale businesses, required changing core sales workflows, and saw low usage after onboarding.
symptoms: nice-to-have workflow tool; low usage; long customer education; changing core order process
evidence_summary: The founder interview clearly states that customers understood the problem but kept postponing adoption because Delite was not urgent enough.
notes: Excellent weak-pain and workflow-change SaaS case.

name: Eloquis
description: A personalization platform for mobile apps.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Rohit Nallapeta said mobile app personalization was too early, not an immediate need, and targeted the wrong customer segment.
symptoms: early mobile personalization market; wrong buyer; weak value articulation; brand confusion
evidence_summary: The founder interview explains that the product assumed a market need before mobile teams were ready to buy this kind of personalization service.
notes: Good early-market/timing case.

name: Eventloot
description: A SaaS platform for wedding planning professionals to manage events and clients.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Justin Anyanwu said Eventloot was built without enough customer input and failed to solve the core problems wedding planners actually had.
symptoms: built assumptions first; Facebook ads poor ROI; users signed up and did not return; missing collaboration features
evidence_summary: The founder interview describes three years of building and marketing before realizing the product lacked the core functionality planners needed.
notes: Strong customer-discovery failure case.

name: Habitual
description: A habit-tracking app inspired by Atomic Habits and built by an experienced solo maker.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Holger Sindbaek said the product was personally useful but failed commercially because marketing and distribution were underestimated in a crowded app category.
symptoms: crowded habit-tracker market; weak marketing; personal itch not enough; app discovery problem
evidence_summary: The founder interview treats Habitual as a commercial failure despite product-building experience because distribution was not solved.
notes: Good solo maker app-distribution case from Denmark.

name: Ink
description: A tool for freelancers to create, send, and sign client contracts.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Andrew Askins said Ink had some traction but the team overbuilt for too long, burned out, and shut down before making the business model work.
symptoms: team burnout; overbuilt product; delayed launch; unclear monetization from free contracts
evidence_summary: The founder interview says the team spent too long building, launched late, and lacked the energy to continue iterating after the initial business model failed.
notes: Good team/burnout and overbuilding case.

name: Muun
description: A SaaS tool for coworking space owners and community managers.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Eelco explained that coworking spaces were budget-constrained, competitors were far ahead, and the product never found a paying segment with enough urgency.
symptoms: well-funded competitors; customers unwilling to pay; low conversion after launch; high churn after pivot
evidence_summary: The founder interview shows a bootstrapped SaaS entering a niche with budget-limited buyers and mature alternatives, making differentiation and pricing difficult.
notes: Good non-US SaaS competition and pricing case.

name: MyCity
description: A SaaS platform for local governments to collect resident feedback and build relationships with citizens.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Stepa Mitaki said MyCity tried to sell software for a process municipalities did not yet have, creating a non-existing market problem.
symptoms: government process did not exist; bottom-up demand failed; direct sales hard; new workflow creation
evidence_summary: The founder interview explains that municipalities lacked internal processes for resident-feedback work, so the SaaS was not improving an existing budgeted workflow.
notes: Strong govtech non-existing-market case.

name: Profitabilly
description: A job-cost tracking SaaS for agencies, consultants, and service businesses.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Natagon shut down Profitabilly despite about $290 MRR because he lacked passion for the market and did not want to run it for years.
symptoms: profitable but low conviction; solo founder motivation loss; limited marketing effort; small customer base
evidence_summary: The founder interview is valuable because it shows a profitable side-project ending from founder-market fit and long-term motivation failure, not pure lack of revenue.
notes: Good unusual case: profitable but not worth continuing.

name: REPitchbook
description: A SaaS tool for real estate professionals to create pitchbooks and marketing materials.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Charlie Reese said REPitchbook failed because pilot customers could not use the product effectively and email integration was missing from their workflow.
symptoms: bad UI; missing email integration; pilot users confused; rebuild required
evidence_summary: The founder interview says pilot feedback exposed major product usability and workflow gaps, and the founder killed the product rather than rebuild without sales traction.
notes: Good pilot-feedback and poor-product-fit SaaS case.

name: RingDaddy
description: A no-code SMS remarketing SaaS for livestreamers to notify audiences when they went live.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Isaac Medeiros learned that streamer audiences were reluctant to give phone numbers, making the core SMS remarketing behavior socially unacceptable.
symptoms: viewer phone-number reluctance; streamer hesitation; negative audience reaction; fast no-code validation
evidence_summary: The founder interview shows a fast MVP that technically worked but failed because the customer/audience behavior required for the product was too uncomfortable.
notes: Strong no-code fast-validation case from Brazil.

name: Tailor
description: An A/B testing and website optimization SaaS for small websites.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Joe d'Elia said Tailor was built without enough research, targeted small sites that could not benefit much from the product, and lacked marketing follow-through.
symptoms: 800 signups but weak onboarding; small sites lacked traffic; broken math; large competitors had free options
evidence_summary: The founder interview connects failure to insufficient validation, weak marketing, and a product whose value was limited for the target customer segment.
notes: Good maker/SaaS distribution case from the UK.

name: sitebot.ai
description: A side-project SaaS chatbot or website assistant product.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Hannu Jaakkola said converting users to customers was challenging and later noted the product lacked a clear target group that would actually buy.
symptoms: unclear target customer; small users unwilling to pay; conversion problems; founder motivation loss
evidence_summary: The Indie Hackers shutdown post and founder comment explain that the product did not have a focused buyer segment with willingness to pay.
notes: Good concise Indie Hackers ICP/pricing case.

name: Tiers
description: A SaaS pricing-testing tool for companies to experiment with pricing flows.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Joshua Anderton said Tiers failed because pricing tests were too infrequent and it was hard to become part of a SaaS company's pricing workflow.
symptoms: infrequent pricing tests; hard workflow insertion; narrow use case; low urgency
evidence_summary: The founder postmortem states that even SaaS companies do not change or test pricing often enough to support a dedicated workflow tool.
notes: Good narrow-use-case SaaS case.

name: DynaDomains
description: A marketplace for leasing valuable domain names to businesses.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Timothy Carambat said DynaDomains listed over $3M worth of domains but had no lessees after ads and outbound failed to produce sales.
symptoms: many domains but no renters; $4K ads with zero sales; 3500 direct emails; domain leasing behavior risk
evidence_summary: The founder postmortem gives unusually concrete failed acquisition data and shows one-sided supply without corresponding demand.
notes: Strong small marketplace liquidity case.

name: Site Pixel
description: A SaaS product for website tracking or on-site pixel-based tools.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Taylor Brazelton shut Site Pixel down after conversations with potential users showed interest but nobody was willing to pay.
symptoms: people talked but did not pay; $50/month perceived expensive; MVP not worth ongoing effort
evidence_summary: The founder postmortem is a concise willingness-to-pay failure: conversations created apparent interest, but customers did not vote with dollars.
notes: Useful tiny SaaS demand-validation case.

name: GrwthApp
description: An Instagram growth automation product for personal brands or social media accounts.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Owen Thurm said GrwthApp could not continue after Instagram banned their own brand account and the founders could no longer guarantee customer account safety.
symptoms: Instagram enforcement risk; customer account safety risk; automation depended on platform tolerance
evidence_summary: The founder postmortem directly ties shutdown to Instagram platform enforcement and inability to safely provide the service to customers.
notes: Strong small platform-risk case.

name: MotionThink
description: A startup building productivity tools for freelancers after an accelerator co-founder matching process.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Andrew Chen said MotionThink shut down because co-founders were not aligned on vision, motivations, and what to build, causing debate to replace execution.
symptoms: co-founder misalignment; long debates; no working product; accelerator team formation risk
evidence_summary: The founder interview gives a detailed account of how mismatched founder motivations and product direction killed the company before it reached a real product launch.
notes: Strong founder-conflict case with detailed primary interview.

name: Observa
description: A YC-backed intrusion detection tool started by a former Robinhood employee.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Rob Picard described joining YC, raising $462k, and shutting down after getting lost in the idea maze without finding a compelling security product wedge.
symptoms: security idea maze; unclear wedge; hard product definition; insufficient customer pull
evidence_summary: The founder interview describes a technically sophisticated security idea that never converged into a focused product with enough customer demand.
notes: Good technical-founder PMF case.

name: WePlate
description: A nutrition optimization app and B2B SaaS for college dining teams.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Alex Hu said WePlate talked to around 100 colleges and learned that neither dining teams nor most students urgently wanted the product.
symptoms: 100 colleges contacted; no buyer pull; students not sufficiently motivated; complex nutrition algorithm without urgent budget
evidence_summary: The founder interview shows a technically thoughtful health product that failed because institutional buyers did not see a bottom-line reason to adopt it.
notes: Strong healthtech/no-urgent-pain case.

name: DenberTech
description: A crypto payment acceptance startup for physical businesses in Spain.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Dennis Ramirez Bernal said crypto hype produced interest but not a real business problem merchants urgently needed solved.
symptoms: crypto hype; high CAC; unfinished MVP; merchants interested in publicity not actual payment need
evidence_summary: The founder interview explains that the startup chased a crypto trend and discovered there was no meaningful pain behind the leads.
notes: Good fintech/crypto hype case from Spain.

name: Star Sync
description: A marketplace selling paid digital experiences with streamers and online creators.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Jonny Boyarsky spent about $95k building before proving market demand, then found creators and users did not adopt the marketplace strongly enough.
symptoms: agency promises failed; creators not invested; low conversion from creator audiences; expensive product build before MVP
evidence_summary: The founder interview is a strong example of overbuilding a marketplace before validating whether either side truly cared enough to transact.
notes: Good marketplace demand-validation case.

name: Cam.ly
description: A Wi-Fi camera hardware startup that tried to stream and store video in the cloud.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Dane Jensen said Cam.ly failed after trying to raise money before proving a polished product in a technically demanding consumer hardware category.
symptoms: unfinished hardware product; cloud video complexity; competing with Nest predecessor; investor skepticism
evidence_summary: The founder interview frames Cam.ly as a hardware/product execution failure: the team needed a polished product before investor conviction could emerge.
notes: Strong early hardware technical-execution case.

name: Wattage
description: A hardware design platform meant to let non-experts customize electronics online.
stated_failure_cause: Failory's cemetery summary says Wattage focused on proving technical feasibility but failed to validate market interest for customizable hardware creation.
symptoms: hardware customization complexity; technical feasibility first; weak validation; investor doubts about scalability
evidence_summary: The case is useful because the technical vision was ambitious, but evidence of customer demand and scalable execution lagged behind the product concept.
notes: Secondary source; keep as a technical/hardware gap filler.

name: Zor Technology
description: A teenage founder's consumer-electronics import and resale business in Australia.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Mathew Carpenter shut Zor Technology down after receiving a legal threat from Apple over imported consumer electronics.
symptoms: Apple legal threat; imported electronics; young founder; legal risk ignored
evidence_summary: The founder interview is a clean small-scale example of legal pressure ending a business even after early sales momentum.
notes: Useful non-US legal-pressure case.

name: MealSurfers
description: A marketplace for home-cooked meals in Canada.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Ali Jiwani said MealSurfers lacked enough recurring customers, faced constant regulatory hurdles, and stayed attached to the original business model too long.
symptoms: Toronto Public Health pressure; less than 10 percent repeat customers; non-standardized home meals; regulatory grey area
evidence_summary: The founder interview explicitly links the soft landing to regulation, weak repeat behavior, and the difficulty of standardizing a home-cooked meal marketplace.
notes: Strong food marketplace regulatory/retention case.

name: Brisk
description: A prescriptive intelligence product that recommended sales actions using CRM data.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Hampus Jakobsson said Brisk became unfocused and depended too heavily on Salesforce's data model, customizations, and roadmap.
symptoms: Salesforce dependency; custom CRM complexity; too many use cases; unfocused onboarding and sales
evidence_summary: The founder interview describes both platform dependency and technical/customer complexity: Brisk tried to support too many Salesforce variants and did not solve one critical problem completely.
notes: Strong European SaaS platform-risk case.

name: Hubrif
description: A Nigerian video-on-demand platform for African short films.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Tobi Ogunwande said Hubrif's niche was too small, monetization did not work, hosting costs were high, and founder/investor disagreements slowed execution.
symptoms: small African short-film audience; zero revenue; hosting costs; disagreement with investor and technical co-founder exit
evidence_summary: The founder interview gives unusually detailed evidence on niche size, failed subscription monetization, and management problems in a non-US media startup.
notes: Excellent Africa/non-US market-size case.

name: Siempo
description: A wellness-focused smartphone interface designed to reduce distraction and improve mental health.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Andrew Murray's interview describes raising over $1M for a phone interface that never generated revenue or reached durable product-market fit.
symptoms: wellness mission; no revenue; hard behavior change; competing with default phone UX
evidence_summary: The founder interview is useful for digital wellness: strong mission and funding were not enough to create a product users would consistently adopt and pay for.
notes: Good mental-health/wellness PMF case.

name: Refolo
description: A plant-based meal-planning app for people trying to improve their diet.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Lola Ojabowale spent two years on Refolo before concluding that the target users were not already spending money to solve the problem.
symptoms: users not already paying; meal planning behavior friction; health interest without buying intent
evidence_summary: The founder interview shows a health/meal-planning product with an appealing mission but weak evidence of willingness to pay.
notes: Good healthtech willingness-to-pay case.

name: Swipes
description: A productivity app company that tried to build task and workflow tools across several years.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Stefan Vladimirov said Swipes operated for six years and nearly raised $1M but never found product-market fit.
symptoms: six-year search; almost raised $1M; no durable PMF; crowded productivity market
evidence_summary: The founder interview is a strong long-running non-US productivity case: persistence and product iterations did not produce a focused market pull.
notes: Good Bulgaria/non-US PMF case.

name: Tali
description: A voice-powered timekeeping assistant for lawyers using Alexa and Google Assistant.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Matthew Volm said Tali burned about $750k while struggling to get meaningful traction for a voice-first legal timekeeping product.
symptoms: voice interface adoption; lawyers' workflow inertia; low traction; expensive build
evidence_summary: The founder interview shows a technically interesting legaltech workflow product that did not fit user behavior strongly enough to justify continued spending.
notes: Good legaltech/voice-interface case.

name: LocalTown
description: A no-code marketplace for makers launching side projects.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Michael Novotny built LocalTown with no-code tools but failed to turn maker interest into a profitable marketplace.
symptoms: maker marketplace; unclear transaction demand; no-code build; weak profitability
evidence_summary: The founder interview is a small marketplace case where interest from makers did not translate into enough monetizable supply-demand liquidity.
notes: Good small no-code marketplace case.

name: Flux
description: A modular multi-messaging client built around owning messages while interacting with mainstream channels.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Jan Johannes said Flux raised about EUR70k plus personal investment but failed after a combination of product, focus, and market issues.
symptoms: multi-channel messaging complexity; data portability vision; open-source aftermath; many combined issues
evidence_summary: The founder interview describes a technically ambitious messaging product where platform integration and product complexity outpaced clear market pull.
notes: Good Germany technical/platform complexity case.

name: Hello Tyro
description: A Belgian HR platform matching students with startup internships.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Pierre Tillement said Hello Tyro reached about EUR4k MRR and raised EUR250k, but still failed to find product-market fit and ran out of cash.
symptoms: HR marketplace/SaaS pivot; modest MRR; annual commitments; bankruptcy
evidence_summary: The founder interview shows a service-to-platform HR startup with some revenue but insufficient PMF to survive after funding ran out.
notes: Good European HR SaaS case.

name: Botnim
description: A web app and chatbot for finding nearby dishes by nutrition values and location.
stated_failure_cause: Founders Shaked Klein Orbach and Gilad Peled said they failed to validate that people eating out cared enough about nutrition filtering to use the product.
symptoms: chatbot latency; map pivot; restaurant lab-testing costs; wrong assumption about eating-out behavior
evidence_summary: The founder interview clearly states that the team built around an assumption: people who cared deeply about nutrition usually did not eat out often enough for the product.
notes: Good Israel health/food PMF case.

name: Singulution
description: A real-time point-of-sale and business management system for multi-location vendors.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Hunt Burdick spent 10 months and about $30k building a complex POS/business-management system before validating market demand.
symptoms: full-duplex real-time POS; multi-location complexity; 10-month build; insufficient lean validation
evidence_summary: The founder interview is a strong lean-startup cautionary case: technical product ambition consumed time and money before market validation.
notes: Good technical overbuilding case.

name: WantRemoteJob
description: A job board for remote jobs in the IT industry.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Vyacheslav Grzhybovsky shut down WantRemoteJob after realizing that maintaining and growing the job board required more time and people than the expected payoff justified.
symptoms: job board maintenance burden; solo founder capacity; insufficient payoff; two-sided marketplace effort
evidence_summary: The founder interview shows a solo-founder marketplace where ongoing operations and distribution requirements exceeded the realistic return from the niche.
notes: Good Latvia/non-US solo marketplace case.

name: Cuddli
description: A dating app for geeks that reached more than 100,000 users.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Robert Walker said Cuddli had users and couples but no realistic path to profitability or exit before the founders ran out of personal runway.
symptoms: 100k users; dating-app churn; monetization depended on partner deals; no path to exit
evidence_summary: The founder interview is valuable because it separates product usage from company viability: Cuddli created matches but could not turn that into a sustainable business model.
notes: Good final-batch case because it is not a generic PMF failure.

name: Community Coders
description: A marketplace-like education services business connecting local companies with high school students for web and marketing projects.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Kaito Cunningham said Community Coders had too many students and not enough paying businesses, with an unsustainable sales cycle and weak differentiated value.
symptoms: too much student supply; not enough business demand; referrals could not scale; service delivery complexity
evidence_summary: The founder interview explicitly describes a supply-demand imbalance and a weak revenue side, making it useful for marketplace liquidity rather than another PMF-only case.
notes: Good Canada education marketplace-liquidity case.

name: ExploreVR
description: A directory and review site intended to become a TripAdvisor-style discovery product for virtual reality experiences.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Andrey Norin said ExploreVR failed because he built before validating the VR supply-demand equation and lacked startup experience.
symptoms: early VR market; weak market research; directory supply-demand unclear; solo-founder inexperience
evidence_summary: The founder interview shows a market-timing and market-research failure in an emerging category where enthusiasm for VR did not yet imply a viable directory business.
notes: Useful timing/market-size case.

name: Onepagetrip
description: A travel itinerary sharing community for planning and sharing trips on one page.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Ana Santos said Onepagetrip had no clear monetization plan, faced huge travel competitors, and should have validated the business model before building.
symptoms: no monetization plan; travel SEO competition; feature idea without business model; solo founder
evidence_summary: The founder interview is a clean example of a community/product idea failing because the business model and distribution strategy were not worked out early enough.
notes: Good Australia/non-US business-model case.

name: Freshconnect
description: An Indian B2B marketplace for fresh agricultural produce connecting farmers and buyers.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Tarun Gupta said Freshconnect reached meaningful revenue but failed to scale because of focus, hiring, operations, and funding challenges in fresh produce.
symptoms: fresh produce operations; focus problems; hiring mistakes; failed funding round; acquihire outcome
evidence_summary: The founder interview adds a non-US agritech marketplace where offline operations and management complexity mattered as much as software or demand.
notes: Strong India/agritech marketplace case.

name: InoVVorX
description: An Indian software company that mixed client-services revenue with attempts to build startup products.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Maxim Dsouza said InoVVorX burned about $400k while losing focus between services work and startup products.
symptoms: services-product split; 25-person team; burn without focus; early India funding difficulty
evidence_summary: The founder interview is useful for the taxonomy because it captures a hybrid agency/startup failure caused by lack of focus and conflicting business models.
notes: Borderline startup-services hybrid, included for non-US lack-of-focus pattern.

name: Bluesmart
description: A smart luggage hardware startup that built connected suitcases with embedded batteries and tracking features.
stated_failure_cause: Bluesmart shut down after major airlines banned smart luggage with non-removable batteries, undermining the core product design and market access.
symptoms: airline battery ban; non-removable battery design; hardware inventory risk; travel infrastructure dependence
evidence_summary: The shutdown is a clear hardware/regulatory case: the product depended on airline acceptance, and a rule change made the design commercially untenable.
notes: Strategic medium-confidence hardware/regulatory case.

name: YOLO
description: An anonymous Snapchat-integrated Q&A app used by teens to receive anonymous messages.
stated_failure_cause: Snap suspended YOLO after a lawsuit connected anonymous messaging to teen bullying and suicide, cutting off the app's core distribution platform.
symptoms: anonymous teen messaging; cyberbullying allegations; Snap suspension; lawsuit pressure
evidence_summary: The case is useful because trust/safety failure and platform dependency are inseparable: moderation risk triggered legal pressure and platform cutoff.
notes: Strategic trust/safety case; source is secondary/legal rather than founder postmortem.

name: Benja
description: An e-commerce and advertising startup whose founder later pleaded guilty to securities, bank, and wire fraud.
stated_failure_cause: Founder Drew Chapin described how fake-it-till-you-make-it behavior crossed into fraud and destroyed the company and his career.
symptoms: fraud; false representations; legal consequences; founder ethics failure
evidence_summary: The founder interview is an unusually direct primary-source account of legal/ethical failure, making it valuable even though it is not a typical market failure.
notes: Use carefully as founder-ethics/legal-pressure case, not as normal PMF failure.

name: The Nerd Cave
description: An Australian community retail space for gamers and hobbyists.
stated_failure_cause: Founder David Desi said The Nerd Cave lost revenue after moving location and competing near other game and hobby stores, eventually running out of funds.
symptoms: location move; local competition; physical community space; fixed costs; limited local demand
evidence_summary: The founder interview adds an offline/community business where location and operations were decisive, broadening the dataset beyond software-only failures.
notes: Included as a final non-US operational case; not a pure software startup.
